title: Quiz: Optimization Problems
description: Test your understanding of QAOA and quantum optimization

questions.question: What does QAOA stand for?
questions.explanation: QAOA stands for Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm, designed to find approximate solutions to combinatorial optimization problems.

questions.question: What are the two types of Hamiltonians alternated in QAOA?
questions.explanation: QAOA alternates between the cost Hamiltonian (encoding the optimization problem) and the mixer Hamiltonian (typically Σ X_i).

questions.question: In MaxCut, what does the cost Hamiltonian measure?

options.text: The edges cut between two partitions

questions.explanation: The MaxCut Hamiltonian counts edges where endpoints are in different partitions, which we want to maximize.

questions.question: QAOA with more layers (higher p) always gives better results on NISQ devices.
questions.explanation: False. More layers increase circuit depth, which on noisy devices leads to more errors that can outweigh the theoretical benefit of higher p.

questions.question: What is warm-starting in QAOA?

options.text: Initializing with a good classical solution

questions.explanation: Warm-starting initializes QAOA from a good classical solution rather than a uniform superposition, potentially improving results.

questions.question: Why does TSP require O(n²) qubits?

options.text: One-hot encoding: each city at each position

questions.explanation: TSP typically uses one-hot encoding with n positions and n cities, requiring n² qubits to encode which city is visited at each position.

questions.question: Currently, how does QAOA compare to classical optimization?

options.text: Classical methods generally outperform QAOA today

questions.explanation: Currently, classical optimization methods (simulated annealing, etc.) generally outperform QAOA on practical problems due to noise and depth limitations.

questions.question: Which are techniques to improve QAOA performance? (Select all)
questions.explanation: Warm-starting, custom mixers, and parameter transfer all help QAOA. Ignoring parameters would make QAOA fail.
